volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Ion (Section 1450 - Section 1570)

sections.label: Section 1450
sections.original: ἐμοὶ γενέσθαι πάντα μᾶλλον ἄν ποτε ,
sections.translation: This is the last thing that ever would have occurred to me , mother , that I was thy child .

sections.label: Section 1452
sections.translation: With fear I tremble still .

sections.label: Section 1453
sections.translation: Dost thou doubt my reality ?

sections.label: Section 1453b
sections.translation: Far from me had I banished these hopes . Whence , O whence , lady , didst thou take my babe

sections.label: Section 1455
sections.original: τίνʼ ἀνὰ χεῖρα δόμον ἔβα Λοξίου ;
sections.translation: into thy arms ? Who carried him to the courts of Loxias ?

sections.label: Section 1456
sections.original: θεῖον τόδʼ · ἀλλὰ τἀπίλοιπα τῆς τύχης
sections.translation: ’ Tis a miracle ! Oh ! may we for the rest of our career be happy , as we were hapless heretofore .

sections.label: Section 1458
sections.translation: In tears wert thou brought forth , my child ,

sections.label: Section 1460
sections.original: νῦν δὲ γενειάσιν παρὰ σέθεν πνέω
sections.translation: and with sorrow to thy mother didst thou leave her arms ; but now I breathe again as I press my lips to thy cheek , in full enjoyment of happiness .

sections.label: Section 1462
sections.original: τοὐμὸν λέγουσα καὶ τὸ σὸν κοινῶς λέγεις .
sections.translation: Thy words express our mutual feelings .

sections.label: Section 1463
sections.translation: No more am I of son and heir bereft ; my house is stablished and my country hath a prince ;

sections.label: Section 1465
sections.translation: Erechtheus groweth young again ; no longer is the house of the earth-born race plunged in gloom , but lifts its eyes unto the radiant sun .

sections.label: Section 1468
sections.original: μῆτερ , παρών μοι καὶ πατὴρ μετασχέτω
sections.translation: Mother mine , since my father too is here , let him share the joy I have brought to thee .

sections.label: Section 1470
sections.translation: My child , my child , what sayst thou ? How is my sin finding me out !

sections.label: Section 1472
sections.translation: What meanest thou ?

sections.label: Section 1472a
sections.translation: Thou art of a different , for different stock .

sections.label: Section 1473
sections.original: ὤμοι · νόθον με παρθένευμʼ ἔτικτε σόν ;
sections.translation: Alas for me ! Am I a bastard , then , bom in thy maiden days ?

sections.label: Section 1474
sections.translation: Nor nuptial torch nor dance ,

sections.label: Section 1475
sections.translation: my child , ushered in my wedding and thy birth .

sections.label: Section 1477
sections.translation: O mother , mother ! whence do I draw my base origin ?

sections.label: Section 1478
sections.translation: Be witness she who slew the Gorgon ,

sections.label: Section 1478b
sections.translation: What meanest thou ?

sections.label: Section 1479
sections.translation: She that on my native rocks

sections.label: Section 1480
sections.translation: makes the olive-clad hill her seat .

sections.label: Section 1481b
sections.original: λέγεις μοι δόλια κοὐ σαφῆ τάδε .
sections.translation: Thy words to me are but as cunning riddles . I cannot read them .

sections.label: Section 1482
sections.translation: Hard by the rock with nightingales melodious , Phoebus ,

sections.label: Section 1483b
sections.translation: Why dost thou mention Phoebus ?

sections.label: Section 1484
sections.translation: Forced on me his secret love .

sections.label: Section 1485
sections.original: λέγʼ · ὡς ἐρεῖς τι κεδνὸν εὐτυχές τέ μοι .
sections.translation: Say on ; for thy story will crown me with fame and fortune .

sections.label: Section 1486
sections.translation: And as the tenth month came round I bore a child to Phoebus in secret .

sections.label: Section 1488
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατʼ εἰποῦσʼ , εἰ λέγεις ἐτήτυμα .
sections.translation: Oh ! thy happy tidings , if thy story is true .

sections.label: Section 1489
sections.translation: And about thee as swaddling-clothes I fastened this my maiden work ,

sections.label: Section 1490
sections.original: σπάργανʼ ἀμφίβολά σοι τάδʼ ἐνῆψα , κερ-
sections.translation: the faulty efforts of my loom . But to my breast I never held thy lips , or suckled or washed thee with a mother ’ s care ; but in a desert cave wert thou cast out

sections.label: Section 1495
sections.translation: to die , for taloned kites to rend and feast upon .

sections.label: Section 1497
sections.translation: An awful deed ! O mother !

sections.label: Section 1497b
sections.translation: Fear held me captive , and I cast thy life away , my child ;

sections.label: Section 1500
sections.translation: I would , though loth , have slain thee too .

sections.label: Section 1500b
sections.original: ἐξ ἐμοῦ τʼ οὐχ ὅσιʼ ἔθνῃσκες .
sections.translation: Thou too wert all but slain by me most impiously .

sections.label: Section 1502
sections.translation: O the horror of all I suffered then ! O the horror of what is to follow now ! To and fro

sections.label: Section 1505
sections.translation: from bad to good we toss , though now the gale is shifting round . May it remain steady ! the past brought sorrows enough ; but now hath a fair breeze sprung up , my son , to waft us out of woe .

sections.label: Section 1510
sections.translation: Let no man ever deem a thing past hoping for , when he turns an eye towards what is happening now .

sections.label: Section 1512
sections.translation: O Fortune ! who ere now hast changed the lot of countless mortals first to grief , and then to joy again , to what a goal my life had come ,

sections.label: Section 1515
sections.translation: even to staining my hands with a mother ’ s blood and enduring sufferings ill-deserved ! Ah well ! may we not learn these truths daily in all that the bright sun embraces ? O mother , in thee have I made a happy discovery , and from my point of view there is no fault to find with my birth ;

sections.label: Section 1520
sections.original: τὰ δʼ ἄλλα πρὸς σὲ βούλομαι μόνην φράσαι .
sections.translation: but what remains I fain would speak to thee apart . Come hither , for I would say a word in thine ear , and o ’ er these matters cast the veil of silence . Bethink thee , mother , carefully ; didst thou make the fatal slip , that maidens will , as touching secret amours ,

sections.label: Section 1525
sections.original: ἔπειτα τῷ θεῷ προστίθης τὴν αἰτίαν ,
sections.translation: and then upon the god wouldst foist the blame , in thy anxiety to escape the shame of my birth asserting that Phoebus is my sire , albeit the god was not the parent .

sections.label: Section 1528
sections.translation: Nay , by our queen of Victory , Athena , that fought by Zeus , in days gone by , high on his car against the earth-born giants I swear ,

sections.label: Section 1530
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστιν ὅστις σοι πατὴρ θνητῶν , τέκνον ,
sections.translation: no mortal is thy father , my son , but King Loxias himself who brought thee up .

sections.label: Section 1532
sections.original: πῶς οὖν τὸν αὑτοῦ παῖδʼ ἔδωκʼ ἄλλῳ πατρὶ
sections.translation: How then is it he gave his own child to another father , declaring that I was begotten of Xuthus ?

sections.label: Section 1534
sections.original: πεφυκέναι μὲν οὐχί , δωρεῖται δέ σε
sections.translation: Begotten he never said , but as a gift he doth bestow thee

sections.label: Section 1535
sections.original: αὑτοῦ γεγῶτα · καὶ γὰρ ἂν φίλος φίλῳ
sections.translation: his own son on him ; for friend might give to friend even his own son to rule his house .

sections.label: Section 1537
sections.original: ὁ θεὸς ἀληθὴς ἢ μάτην μαντεύεται ,
sections.translation: Mother mine , this thought disturbs my breast , as well it may , whether the god speaks truth or gives an idle oracle .

sections.label: Section 1539
sections.original: ἄκουε δή νυν ἅμʼ ἐσῆλθεν , ὦ τέκνον ·
sections.translation: Hear , then , my son , the thought that hath occurred to me ;

sections.label: Section 1540
sections.translation: Loxias out of kindness is establishing thee in a noble family , for hadst thou been called the god ’ s son , thou hadst never inherited a father ’ s home and name . How couldst thou , when I strove to hide my marriage with him and would have slain thee privily ?

sections.label: Section 1545
sections.original: ὃ δʼ ὠφελῶν σε προστίθησʼ ἄλλῳ πατρί .
sections.translation: But he for thy interest is handing thee over to another father .

sections.label: Section 1546
sections.original: οὐχ ὧδε φαύλως αὔτʼ ἐγὼ μετέρχομαι ,
sections.translation: Not thus lightly do I pursue the inquiry ; nay , I will enter Apollo ’ s shrine and question him whether I am the child of a mortal sire or his own son . Ha ! who is that hovering o ’ er the incense-smoking roof ,

sections.label: Section 1550
sections.translation: and showing to our gaze a heavenly face , bright as the sun ? Let us fly , mother , that we see not sights divine , unless haply it is right we should .

sections.label: Section 1553
sections.original: μὴ φεύγετʼ · οὐ γὰρ πολεμίαν με φεύγετε ,
sections.translation: Fly not ! I am no foe ye seek to shun , but alike in Athens and this place your kindly friend .

sections.label: Section 1555
sections.translation: ’ Tis I , Pallas , after whom your land is named , that am here , by Apollo sent in headlong haste ; for he thought not fit to appear before you twain , lest his coming might provoke reproaches for the past ; but me he sends to proclaim to you his words ,

sections.label: Section 1560
sections.original: ὡς ἥδε τίκτει σʼ ἐξ Ἀπόλλωνος πατρός ,
sections.translation: how that this is thy mother , and Apollo thy sire ; while thyself he doth bestow , as seems him good , not indeed on him that begat thee , nay , but that he may bring thee to a house of high repute . For when this matter was brought to light , he devised a way of deliverance ,

sections.label: Section 1565
sections.original: καὶ τήνδε πρὸς σοῦ , μηχαναῖς ἐρρύσατο .
sections.translation: fearing that thou wouldst be slain by thy mother ’ s wiles and she by thine . Now it was King Apollo ’ s wish to keep this matter secret awhile , and then in Athens to acknowledge this lady as thy mother and thyself as the child of her and Phoebus . But to end the business and discharge his oracles for the god ,

sections.label: Section 1570
sections.translation: I bid you hearken ; for such was my purpose in yoking my chariot-steeds .
